description.summary: The most famous of three Gorgon sisters in Greek mythology, known for snake hair and petrifying gaze. Once a beautiful woman cursed by Athena, beheaded by Perseus.
description.full: Medusa is the most famous of the three Gorgon sisters in Greek mythology, distinguished by her mortal nature while her sisters Stheno and Euryale were immortal. Characterized by living venomous snake hair, bronze hands, golden wings, boar tusks, and a petrifying gaze that turned anyone who looked directly at her to stone. According to later versions, Medusa was originally a beautiful woman cursed by Athena after being raped by Poseidon in Athena's temple. She was slain by the hero Perseus, who used the reflection in his polished shield to avoid her deadly gaze and beheaded her. From her blood sprang the winged horse Pegasus and the warrior Chrysaor. Her severed head retained its petrifying power and was given to Athena, who placed it on her shield, the Aegis. Medusa has become a powerful cultural symbol representing both the apotropaic (warding off evil), female rage, and the victim/monster duality. Modern feminist interpretations reclaim her as a symbol of survival and power rather than monstrosity.

etymology.origin: From Greek 'Medousa,' from 'medein' meaning 'to protect' or 'to rule'

related_phenomena.name: Gorgons
related_phenomena.description: Medusa is one of three Gorgon sisters

related_phenomena.name: Pegasus
related_phenomena.description: Winged horse born from Medusa's blood

features.description: Living venomous serpents instead of hair
Eyes that turn onlookers to stone

physical.morphology: Monstrous hybrid form with serpentine and bestial features. Earlier art depicts her as grotesque; later art sometimes shows her as beautiful with snakes for hair.
physical.mortality: Mortal, unlike her immortal sisters Stheno and Euryale

abilities.description: Anyone who looks directly at her face turns to stone instantly
Head retains petrifying power even after death

behavior.habitat: Remote locations at edge of world

range.description: Greek mythology; dwelling at edge of known world

origins.cultural_roots: Greek mythology; Gorgons appear in earliest Greek art and literature
origins.earliest_accounts: Hesiod's Theogony (c. 700 BCE); Homer's Iliad references Gorgon head
origins.evolution: Evolved from purely monstrous to tragic figure with victim narrative

timeline.significance: Early account of Gorgons including Medusa
Introduces origin story of Medusa as beautiful woman cursed by Athena
Medusa's face used as protective symbol on shields, buildings, armor

origin_story.early_version: Born as monster, daughter of primordial sea deities Phorcys and Ceto
origin_story.ovid_version: Beautiful maiden who served in Athena's temple; raped by Poseidon in the temple; cursed by Athena and transformed into monster
origin_story.interpretation: Ovid's version introduces victim narrative; punishment for being raped reflects victim-blaming

perseus_myth.quest: Perseus tasked with bringing back Medusa's head by King Polydectes
divine_aid: Athena gave him polished bronze shield (to see reflection)
Hades gave him helm of invisibility
perseus_myth.strategy: Used shield as mirror to avoid direct gaze; beheaded her while she slept
perseus_myth.birth_from_blood: From her neck sprang Pegasus (winged horse) and Chrysaor (golden sword warrior), both fathered by Poseidon
perseus_myth.aftermath: Head given to Athena, who placed it on her shield (Aegis) as protection

head_as_weapon.perseus_use: Perseus used the severed head to petrify enemies including sea monster Cetus and King Polydectes
head_as_weapon.athena_aegis: Placed on Athena's shield as ultimate defensive weapon and symbol of power
head_as_weapon.apotropaic_function: Image of Medusa (gorgoneion) used throughout ancient Greece to ward off evil

genealogy.siblings: Stheno, Euryale, and other monstrous beings
genealogy.offspring: Pegasus and Chrysaor (born from her blood after beheading)

artistic.name: Greek vases
artistic.description: Numerous depictions of Perseus and Medusa

artistic.name: Medusa Rondanini
artistic.date: Roman copy of 5th c. BCE Greek original

artistic.name: Cellini's Perseus with the Head of Medusa

symbolic_interpretations.name: Apotropaic symbol
symbolic_interpretations.description: Medusa's face wards off evil; frightening image protects from harm
evidence_cited: Widespread use on shields, buildings, armor

symbolic_interpretations.name: Female rage and power
symbolic_interpretations.description: Represents female anger and power that men find threatening

symbolic_interpretations.name: Victim/monster duality
symbolic_interpretations.description: Embodies transformation from victim to monster; punished for being victimized

symbolic_interpretations.name: Chthonic power
symbolic_interpretations.description: Represents primordial, earth-based power that must be controlled

feminist_interpretations.reclamation: Modern feminists reclaim Medusa as symbol of female rage, survival, and power
feminist_interpretations.survivor_symbol: Represents survivors of sexual violence transforming trauma into strength
feminist_interpretations.critique: Ovid's narrative seen as victim-blaming; Athena punishes victim rather than rapist Poseidon

ancient_symbolism.apotropaic: Primary function was to ward off evil; 'evil eye' protection
ancient_symbolism.power: Symbol of ultimate power and protection (on Athena's shield)
ancient_symbolism.transformation: Death and rebirth (offspring from her blood)

modern_symbolism.feminist_icon: Reclaimed as symbol of female rage and survivor strength
modern_symbolism.literary: Symbol of dangerous beauty, transformation, othering
modern_symbolism.psychological: Represents facing one's fears, shadow self

visual_arts: Rubens, da Vinci, and countless other artists

modern_usage.survivor_symbol: Used by sexual assault survivors as symbol of transformation and power
modern_usage.feminist_rallying_point: Symbol of female rage against patriarchal violence
modern_usage.cultural_commentary: Represents societal treatment of victims, especially women

sources.title: Medusa: Solving the Mystery of the Gorgon
